claim_chain: Systematic review of 46 TB prevalence surveys from 26 countries representing 4.6 billion people
TB epidemics becoming increasingly urbanised in proportional and absolute terms
Driven by rapidly urbanising demographic trends combined with epidemiological shifts
Regional heterogeneity with Africa showing earlier urbanisation than Western Pacific and South-East Asia
Urban-to-rural prevalence ratio increased over 24 years, particularly for smear-positive disease
Public health strategies must be tailored to local urban and rural TB epidemiology
Essential for accelerating progress toward End TB Strategy targets

typical_pattern: State main finding with quantitative evidence
Connect to policy or public health implications

likely_priorities: Clarity of urban-rural definitions across heterogeneous settings
Robustness of time trend estimates given survey spacing
Validity of population estimates from UN and World Bank sources
likely_attacks: No unified global definition of urban and rural areas used
Only two studies from Eastern Mediterranean Region with markedly different estimates
Population estimates may misestimate growth and urbanisation rates
Absence of data from Americas and Europe limits global conclusions
Heterogeneity in survey methods and screening algorithms across studies
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The limitations paragraph explicitly states: 'There is no unified global definition of urban and rural areas, and countries and regions often use varying criteria'; 'To estimate urban and rural TB burden, we used population estimates from the United Nations and World Bank; these may misestimate population growth, and particular rates of urbanisation'; 'Only two studies were available from the Eastern Mediterranean Region (Pakistan and Sudan), each providing markedly different estimates'; 'The main limitations include lack of unified definitions for urban and rural areas, and absence of data for some global regions (e.g., Americas and Europe)'. These self-identified limitations form the basis for inferring likely reviewer concerns about definitions, data gaps, and generalizability.

likely increased over the 24 years during which surveys were conducted
This trend cannot be explained by population redistribution alone
These findings suggest that demographic and epidemiological processes are acting in concert
will be essential for accelerating progress toward the End TB Strategy targets

phrase_evidence.phrase: In this systematic review and meta-analysis of 46 national and sub-national TB prevalence surveys from 26 low- and -income countries
we found that TB epidemics are becoming increasingly urbanised in both proportional and absolute terms
driven by rapidly urbanising demographic trends
emphasising the need to accelerate efforts to interrupt TB transmission within cities
These findings highlight the importance of understanding local TB epidemiology to tailor public health strategies to context
which will be essential for accelerating progress toward the End TB Strategy targets
However, this overall pattern masked substantial regional heterogeneity
likely increased over the 24 years during which surveys were conducted
This trend cannot be explained by population redistribution alone
These findings suggest that demographic and epidemiological processes are acting in concert
Regional patterns revealed substantial heterogeneity in timing of the shift of relative burden
with a 83% posterior probability the urban-to-rural prevalence ratio exceeded one in 2024
is likely to reflect the combined influence of overlapping epidemics and demographic changes
Taken together, these dynamics suggest that TB control strategies must be informed by both demographic change and epidemiological context
This study has several limitations
There is no unified global definition of urban and rural areas
these may misestimate population growth, and particular rates of urbanisation
Only two studies were available from the Eastern Mediterranean Region
The main limitations include lack of unified definitions for urban and rural areas, and absence of data for some global regions
Coinciding with these rapid demographic changes
In other words
In contrast
At the same time
Green points are crude survey urban-to-rural prevalence ratios, and black points and error bars posterior means and 95% credible intervals
Points are crude prevalence survey estimates at survey midpoint
Estimated from a Bayesian multivariate regression model
creates environments conducive to the transmission of infectious diseases, including TB
with the rise being particularly pronounced for smear-positive disease
reflecting differences in demographic change, health system capacity, and social conditions
addressing the needs of urban poor populations—particularly in informal settlements—while also strengthening rural primary care
